content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

August 13, 1991


Ann Mills
Commission for Economic Development
State Capitol, Suite 1114
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-91-344

Dear Ms. Mills:

You have requested advice on behalf of the executive director
of the Commission for Economic Development with respect to the
application of the mass mailing provisions of the Political Reform
Act (the "Act").1

QUESTION

Do the mass mailing provisions of the Act apply to state
boards and commissions in the same manner as the provisions apply
to other governmental agencies?

CONCLUSION

The mass mailing provisions of the Act are applicable to all
state and local governmental agencies in California, including
state commissions such as the Commission for Economic Development.

FACTS

The Commission for Economic Development (CED) is considering
the possibility of publishing a newsletter which would be
distributed in quantities of over 200 per month. The executive
director would like to know if such a publication would be subject
to the mass mailing provisions of the Act.


1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

ANALYSIS

Section 89001 states that "no newsletter or other mass mail-
ing shall be sent at public expense." A mass mailing is defined
as two hundred or more substantially similar pieces of mail
(Section 82041.5). Applied literally, this section would prohibit
all mass mailing involving public funds, irrespective of content
or purpose, including such items as tax notices, college
schedules, sample ballots and other mass mailings sent by govern-
ment agencies. Regulation 18901 clarifies that application of the
prohibition is limited to items sent which include references to
elected officers affiliated with the agency which produces or
sends the mailing.2

The basic prohibition is set forth in subdivision (a) of
Regulation 18901 and is as follows:

     (a) Except as provided in subdivision (b)3, a
mailing is prohibited by Section 89001 if all of the
following criteria are met:

     (1) Any item sent is delivered, by any means, to
the recipient at his or her residence, place of employ-
ment or business, or post office box. For purposes of
this subdivision (a)(1), the item delivered to the
recipient must be a tangible item, such as a videotape,
record, or button, or a written document.

     (2) The item sent either:

     (A) Features an elected officer affiliated with
the agency which produces or sends the mailing, or

     (B) Includes the name, office, photograph, or
other reference to an elected officer affiliated with
the agency which produces or sends the mailing, and is
prepared or sent in cooperation, consultation, co-
ordination, or concert with the elected officer.

     (3)(A) Any of the costs of distribution is paid
for with public moneys; or

     (B) Costs of design, production, and printing
exceeding $50.00 are paid with public moneys, and the
design, production, or printing is done with the intent

2    The Commission's authority to interpret Section 89001 to avoid
the absurd results of its literal application was upheld in Watson
v. Fair Political Practices Com., (1990) 217 Cal.App.3d 1059.

3    Subdivision (b) of Regulation 18901 sets forth a series of
limited exceptions to the general prohibition.

of sending the item other than as permitted by this
regulation.

     (4) More than two hundred substantially similar
items are sent in a single calendar month, excluding
any item sent in response to an unsolicited request and
any item described in subdivision (b).

                                          Emphasis added.

     Regulation 18901 is applicable to items sent by any
governmental agency, but only limits mailings of the agency which
include the name, photograph or other reference to an elected
officer who is affiliated with that agency.

     The Act defines an agency as including "any state agency or
local government agency." (Section 82004.) "State agency" is
defined as "every state office, department, division, bureau,
board and commission, and the Legislature." (Section 82049,
emphasis added.) This definition would include CED, and,
therefore, the provisions of Regulation 18901 are applicable to
CED.

     Based upon information previously provided by your office, it
appears that the chair of CED and several of its commissioners are
elected state officers. (Section 82021.) Elected officers who
are members of an agency are considered to be "affiliated" with
the agency. (Regulation 18901(c)(1).)

     Since the newsletter you describe would be sent to more than
200 persons in a calendar month, it would be considered a mass
mailing. If it is going to be sent at public expense to persons
at their home, office, place of employment or post office box, it
may not include the name, office, photograph or other reference to
the elected officers affiliated with CED unless otherwise
permitted by Regulation 18901.

     For your reference, I am enclosing a copy of Regulation 18901
together with a general information sheet outlining the basic
prohibitions and exceptions.

If you have any questions, please contact me at (916) 322-
5901.4

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin,
Acting General Counsel

By: Margaret W. Ellison
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:MWE:dg

4
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.
